attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents with a two month history of leg swelling, facial swelling and frothy urine. He also feels more tired than usual. He has no shortness of breath, nausea or vomiting. His history includes chronic hepatitis B (on tenofovir) and hypertension (on ramipril).

On examination, his blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg, pulse 80/min and temperature 37 °C. He appears well with bilateral periorbital oedema and bilateral pitting oedema to the knees, with markedly oedematous feet and ankles. He has no ascites, and his chest is clear. His heart sounds are dual with no murmurs.

On urine dipstick, he has 4+ proteinuria.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely finding on electron microscopy of the kidney biopsy?
options: Mesangial deposits suggestive of IgA nephropathy
Intra membranous deposits on glomerular basement membrane
Subepithelial deposits on glomerular basement membrane
attributes.explanation:
Sub-epithelial deposits on glomerular basement membrane are seen in membranous nephropathy, as well as in some cases of lupus nephritis. Subepithelial immune deposits cause thickening of the GBM and can lead to nephrotic syndrome. On electron microscopy, subepithelial immune deposits can be seen protruding into the glomerular capillary lumina. The clinical presentation and laboratory findings in this case are most consistent with a diagnosis of mesangiocapillary glomerulonephritis (MCGN). The patient has a history of hepatitis B, which is a risk factor for the development of membranous nephropathy. Ramipril is an ACE inhibitor which is useful to reduce proteinuria in membranous nephropathy.

IgA nephropathy is a common form of glomerulonephritis that typically presents with haematuria, proteinuria, and sometimes nephrotic syndrome. It is caused by deposition of IgA immune complexes in the mesangium of the glomeruli, leading to inflammation and damage to the glomerular capillary walls. The characteristic finding on kidney biopsy is mesangial deposits suggestive of IgA nephropathy, which can be visualized by immunofluorescence microscopy.

Thin glomerular basement membrane is in which the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) is thinner than normal due to a mutation in the genes encoding the type IV collagen proteins that make up the GBM. Patients with thin GBM disease may have asymptomatic haematuria, proteinuria, or rarely, nephrotic syndrome. On electron microscopy, the GBM appears thin and may be difficult to distinguish from the adjacent endothelial cell basement membrane.

Lipid accumulation in the glomeruli can be seen in a variety of conditions, including diabetic nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and membranous nephropathy. The presence of lipid droplets or lipid-laden cells on electron microscopy is suggestive of this diagnosis. However, the clinical presentation and other laboratory findings in this case do not strongly support these diagnoses.

Intra-membranous deposits on glomerular basement membrane are seen in dense deposit disease (DDD), but this is a relatively rare condition that typically presents in childhood or early adulthood. It is not usually associated with hypertension or hepatitis B.
learningPoints: Membranous nephropathy often presents with nephrotic syndrome
Membranous nephropathy is associated with hepatitis B
references: Alok A, Yadav A. Membranous Nephropathy. [Updated 2022 Jun 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-.
